summary.title: Scenario 3 - Microservices-Based Healthcare Application
summary.description: A healthcare organisation microservices patient management system comprising Patient Portal, Identity Service, Medical Records Service, Appointment Service, Notification Service, and API Gateway. The patient portal and API gateway are exposed to the public internet. All inter-service communication occurs over an internal network.

data.name: Public Internet
data.description: Untrusted external zone where the patient accesses the portal from their browser. All traffic from this zone must be treated as hostile until authenticated.

data.name: DMZ (Perimeter Zone)
data.description: Demilitarised zone containing the Patient Portal and API Gateway, which are the only components exposed to the public internet. Acts as the security perimeter separating untrusted internet traffic from internal services.

data.name: Internal Services Zone
data.description: Trusted internal network zone hosting all backend microservices. Not directly accessible from the public internet. All inbound traffic must pass through the API Gateway.

data.name: Identity Service Boundary
data.description: Isolated trust boundary for the Identity Service, which issues and validates JWTs. Strict access controls limit which services may call this component.

data.name: Medical Records Service Boundary
data.description: Isolated trust boundary for the Medical Records Service, which stores and retrieves highly sensitive patient health information. Token verification is mandatory for all access.

data.name: Appointment Service Boundary
data.description: Isolated trust boundary for the Appointment Service, which manages patient appointment scheduling and reads data from the Medical Records Service.

data.name: Notification Service Boundary
data.description: Isolated trust boundary for the Notification Service, which dispatches appointment reminders via third-party SMS and email providers.

data.name: Third-Party Providers Zone
data.description: External untrusted zone containing third-party SMS and email delivery providers used by the Notification Service. Communications crossing this boundary must be encrypted and validated.

data.name: Patient
data.description: End user accessing the healthcare portal from a browser over the public internet to view medical records and book appointments

data.name: Patient Portal
data.description: Web frontend exposed to the public internet. Patients use it to log in, view medical records, and book appointments. Communicates with the API Gateway for all backend operations.

threats.title: Patient Portal Phishing and Credential Harvesting
threats.description: An attacker creates a convincing replica of the patient portal login page and tricks patients into submitting their credentials via phishing emails or malicious links. Captured credentials are then used to impersonate legitimate patients and access sensitive medical records.
threats.mitigation: Enforce multi-factor authentication for all patient accounts. Implement HSTS and certificate pinning to prevent navigation to lookalike domains. Provide patient security awareness guidance. Monitor for login anomalies such as unusual geolocations or devices.

threats.title: Cross-Site Scripting on Patient Portal
threats.description: An attacker injects malicious JavaScript into the patient portal through unsanitised user input fields such as appointment notes or profile fields. When other patients or administrators view the affected pages, the script executes in their browser, stealing session tokens or redirecting to malicious sites.
threats.mitigation: Implement a strict Content Security Policy. Sanitise and HTML-encode all user-supplied output server-side. Use HttpOnly and Secure cookie flags on all session cookies. Perform regular penetration testing of all frontend input fields.

data.name: API Gateway
data.description: Single entry point for all inbound requests to internal microservices. Routes authenticated requests to appropriate backend services and enforces access control policies.

threats.title: API Gateway DDoS Rendering All Services Unavailable
threats.description: An attacker floods the API Gateway with a high volume of requests, exhausting its connection pool and processing capacity. Since all internal services are routed exclusively through the gateway, a successful attack renders the entire patient management system unavailable, preventing patients from accessing medical records or booking urgent appointments.
threats.mitigation: Deploy rate limiting and throttling per client IP and per authenticated user. Integrate a cloud-based DDoS mitigation service in front of the gateway. Implement auto-scaling to absorb legitimate traffic spikes. Define circuit breakers to protect backend services from cascading failures.

threats.title: Patient Data Exposure via API Gateway Misconfiguration
threats.description: A misconfigured routing rule or overly permissive API Gateway policy inadvertently exposes internal medical records or identity service endpoints directly to the public internet without authentication checks. An attacker enumerating API paths could retrieve protected health information without any valid credentials.
threats.mitigation: Apply a whitelist-only approach to API Gateway routing rules, denying all unlisted paths by default. Enforce JWT validation at the gateway layer before routing any request. Conduct regular gateway configuration audits. Use automated security scanning to detect unprotected endpoints.

data.name: Identity Service
data.description: Handles authentication and authorisation using OAuth 2.0. Issues signed JSON Web Tokens to authenticated users. All internal services verify tokens against this service.

threats.title: JWT Token Forgery via Weak Signing Key
threats.description: If the Identity Service uses a weak or default secret key to sign JWTs, an attacker who discovers the key through brute force or source code exposure can forge arbitrary tokens granting themselves any patient identity or administrative role. All services that accept these tokens without proper verification would then process malicious requests as authenticated.
threats.mitigation: Use RS256 asymmetric signing for JWTs with a minimum 2048-bit RSA key pair. Store private keys in a hardware security module or secrets manager. Rotate signing keys periodically and support key versioning. Implement short JWT expiry times and token refresh flows. All services must validate the token signature, issuer, audience, and expiry on every request.

threats.title: OAuth Scope Escalation via Identity Service
threats.description: An attacker manipulates the OAuth 2.0 authorisation request to the Identity Service, requesting broader scopes than they are entitled to, for example requesting administrative or write access to medical records when only read access to their own appointments should be granted. If the Identity Service does not strictly validate and enforce scope boundaries, the resulting JWT may grant excessive permissions across all internal services.
threats.mitigation: Enforce strict server-side scope validation in the Identity Service, ignoring client-requested scopes not explicitly pre-approved for the client application. Implement principle of least privilege for all OAuth scopes. Log all token issuance events with requested and granted scopes. Conduct regular authorisation model reviews.

data.name: Medical Records Service
data.description: Stores and retrieves patient medical records including diagnoses, prescriptions, and test results. Verifies JWTs with the Identity Service before processing any request.

threats.title: Unauthorised Medical Record Access via BOLA
threats.description: The Medical Records Service does not enforce object-level authorisation checks, allowing an authenticated patient to access other patients records simply by modifying the patient ID parameter in a request. This Broken Object Level Authorisation vulnerability enables mass exposure of protected health information for any patient in the system.
threats.mitigation: Implement mandatory object-level authorisation checks for every record retrieval and modification operation, verifying that the requesting user identity in the JWT matches the patient ID of the requested record. Apply OWASP API Security Top 10 controls. Conduct regular authorisation penetration testing.

threats.title: Medical Record Tampering via Compromised Service Account
threats.description: An attacker who compromises an internal service account or a co-located microservice can send authenticated requests to the Medical Records Service to modify or delete patient records, alter diagnoses, remove prescriptions, or corrupt test results. Without write integrity controls, the tampered data is indistinguishable from legitimate records.
threats.mitigation: Enforce role-based access control distinguishing read-only patient access from write access reserved for authorised clinical staff. Implement cryptographic signing of medical record entries to detect tampering. Enable immutable audit logging for all create, update, and delete operations. Follow NIST 800-53 SI-7 software and information integrity controls.

threats.title: Absence of Medical Record Access Audit Logs
threats.description: Without comprehensive immutable audit logging, a malicious insider who accesses or modifies patient medical records can deny having done so. The absence of a verifiable audit trail also makes it impossible to satisfy regulatory requirements such as HIPAA audit controls, investigate data breaches, or detect patterns of unauthorised access.
threats.mitigation: Implement append-only audit logging for all access and modification events on medical records, capturing user identity, timestamp, action performed, record identifier, and source IP. Store logs in a separate tamper-evident log store. Retain logs for a minimum period as required by applicable healthcare regulations. Align with ISO 27001 A.12.4 logging and monitoring controls.

data.name: Appointment Service
data.description: Manages patient appointment scheduling and cancellations. Reads patient data from the Medical Records Service and triggers appointment reminders via the Notification Service.

threats.title: Appointment Modification without Non-Repudiation
threats.description: The Appointment Service does not maintain a verifiable record of who booked, modified, or cancelled an appointment and at what time. A malicious actor such as a rogue staff member could cancel critical patient appointments and subsequently deny responsibility, with no audit evidence to contradict their claim. This poses both a patient safety risk and a regulatory compliance failure.
threats.mitigation: Log all appointment creation, modification, and cancellation events with the authenticated user identity, timestamp, and change details in an immutable audit trail. Implement digital signatures on appointment records to provide cryptographic non-repudiation. Align with HIPAA audit control requirements under 45 CFR 164.312.

data.name: Notification Service
data.description: Sends appointment reminders to patients via SMS and email using third-party provider APIs. Receives notification requests from the Appointment Service.

threats.title: Patient PHI Leakage in Notification Payloads
threats.description: The Notification Service includes excessive patient health information such as diagnosis details or medication names in SMS or email notification payloads sent to third-party providers. Once this data crosses the trust boundary to an external provider, the organisation loses control over it. A breach at the third-party provider exposes sensitive protected health information in violation of HIPAA.
threats.mitigation: Enforce data minimisation in all outbound notifications, including only the minimum information necessary such as appointment date and time. Never include diagnosis, treatment, or medication details in notification content. Encrypt notification payloads in transit using TLS 1.2 or higher. Review third-party provider data processing agreements for HIPAA compliance. Apply NIST 800-53 SC-8 transmission confidentiality controls.

threats.title: Notification Service Abuse for SMS or Email Flooding
threats.description: An attacker who gains access to an authenticated session or exploits a vulnerability in the Appointment Service can trigger the Notification Service to send large volumes of SMS or email messages to arbitrary recipients, exhausting third-party provider quotas and API rate limits. This disables legitimate appointment reminders for all patients and may incur significant financial cost.
threats.mitigation: Implement strict per-user and per-appointment rate limits on notification dispatch. Validate that notification recipients match the patient identity in the authenticated JWT before sending. Monitor third-party provider API usage in real time and alert on anomalous spikes. Use asynchronous message queues with backpressure controls to absorb demand spikes.

data.name: SMS Provider
data.description: Third-party external SMS delivery service used by the Notification Service to send appointment reminder text messages to patients

data.name: Email Provider
data.description: Third-party external email delivery service used by the Notification Service to send appointment confirmation and reminder emails to patients

data.name: Portal Access (HTTPS)
data.description: Patient accesses the portal login page and submits credentials over HTTPS from the public internet

data.name: API Request (HTTPS)
data.description: Patient Portal forwards authenticated patient requests to the API Gateway for routing to backend services

data.name: Token Validation Request
data.description: API Gateway forwards JWT to the Identity Service for signature and claims verification before routing the request

data.name: JWT / Validation Result
data.description: Identity Service returns validated claims and authorisation result to the API Gateway

data.name: Medical Record Request
data.description: API Gateway routes authenticated patient medical record read request to the Medical Records Service

data.name: Verify JWT Token
data.description: Medical Records Service independently verifies the JWT token with the Identity Service before returning patient data

data.name: Patient Record Response
data.description: Medical Records Service returns requested patient health data to the API Gateway for forwarding to the portal

data.name: Appointment Request
data.description: API Gateway routes authenticated appointment booking or viewing request to the Appointment Service

data.name: Read Patient Data
data.description: Appointment Service retrieves relevant patient data from the Medical Records Service to support appointment scheduling

data.name: Trigger Reminder Notification
data.description: Appointment Service sends appointment details to the Notification Service to dispatch reminder messages to the patient

data.name: SMS Reminder (HTTPS API)
data.description: Notification Service sends appointment reminder SMS request to the third-party SMS provider via HTTPS API

data.name: Email Reminder (SMTP/API)
data.description: Notification Service sends appointment reminder email request to the third-party email provider via SMTP or HTTPS API

data.name: Routed Response
data.description: API Gateway forwards backend service response back to the Patient Portal for display to the patient

data.name: Portal Response (HTTPS)
data.description: Patient Portal returns requested information or confirmation to the patient browser over HTTPS
